next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

By four-thirty the heat had not left the garage apartment. It sat in the block walls with the bleach sting from yesterday's mop water and the old frying oil that never quite washed out of the curtains. Delroy dressed in the dark until the shoulder made that impossible. Then he clicked on the stove light and worked his right arm into a clean shirt one inch at a time, jaw locked, the cuff brushing the enamel sink because he could not lift higher without the whole side of him going hot and mean.

On the table, under the edge of the rice tin so Micah would not knock it down, lay Tiana's document folder. Blue plastic, split at one corner, tabbed with index-card strips cut so straight they looked measured. Birth certificate copy. School letters. Aid printouts. The stack had the neat, desperate look of something that had already been asked to prove too much. Delroy kept his eyes on it long enough for the pain in his shoulder to get mixed up with the other thing, the older thing, the one that had not stopped working on him since the night he admitted where her document money had gone and tore the house open. He had taken money from that future and turned it into rent, light, groceries, bus fare, days bought one at a time. Necessary at the time. Shameful after. Both things could sit in the same chair and stare at him.

Marcia spoke before he heard her move. "You leaving now?"

Her voice came from the bed, flat with tiredness rather than sleep. She had not been resting anyway. Celia had been dead less than two days. Grief and money had crowded the room so completely the...
open_hooks_to_carry: Virginia Key feels like genuine bodily relief.
Tiana and Micah briefly recover sibling ease.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...this morning. Nothing in Marcia's expression said surprise. Nothing in it said approval either. There was only recognition, tired and sharp.

"You come in early," Marcia said.

Tiana set her folder down by the chair. "Yeah."

Marcia took a sip of coffee. "You alright?"

It was not a small question. It held the whole room under it. Tiana could have answered with the truth in parts. I'm furious. I'm ashamed. I'm relieved. I'm split open in ways that have nothing to do with my father and also everything to do with him. I spent the night with Malik and I don't know whether that was brave or selfish or both.

Instead she said, "I was with a friend."

Marcia's mouth tightened, not because she believed it and not because she didn't. It tightened because there was no room left to litigate one more concealment before seven in the morning.

"Alright," she said.

That was all. There was no sermon, no demand, just the acceptance of another fact she did not have the luxury to inspect yet.

Tiana stood there with her bag still on her shoulder. The folder on the chair seemed to watch her. Somewhere in the main house, a child started crying for cereal. The standing fan pushed the coffee smell around. Marcia bent back over the legal pad and wrote down a number, crossed it out, wrote another.

Nothing had been solved. The card was still expired. UF was still on hold. Rent would still come due whether people had lied or fucked or prayed or not. The house had only gained one more secret, one more thing not broken exactly but no longer innocent.

Tiana pulled out her chair and sat down anyway.
state_deltas_required: Manufacture one ordinary family day before the house turns into nothing but problem-solving.
Marcia loses her steadiest job, and Delroy quietly commits to Claude's demolition job.
